title: Internal Radiation (Sealed-Source Brachytherapy) Safety: Protecting the Patient and Staff
subtitle: A 54-year-old woman has a sealed intracavitary implant placed for cervical cancer and is on strict bedrest. The nurse applies ALARA — time, distance, and shielding — and knows exactly what to do if the source dislodges.

patient.name: Diane Foster
patient.admitDate: Oncology unit for low-dose-rate intracavitary brachytherapy (tandem and ovoids) for cervical cancer. Sealed source in place; strict bedrest with a Foley and low-residue diet. Private room with radiation signage; lead container and long-handled forceps at the bedside.

vitals.temp: Source found on linens — exposure risk

labs.r: Lead container + long forceps at bedside.

nursesNote: Diane Foster, 54, with a sealed intracavitary brachytherapy implant for cervical cancer; strict bedrest, Foley, low-residue diet in a private room with radiation signage, a lead container, and long-handled forceps at the bedside. Sealed source means her body fluids are NOT radioactive. Care follows ALARA — minimize time, maximize distance, use shielding — with dosimetry badges, visitor screening, and a clear dislodged-source protocol.
providerOrders: Strict bedrest with Foley; low-residue diet while implant is in place
Private room with radiation warning signage; staff wear dosimetry badges
Apply ALARA: minimize time, maximize distance, use shielding; rotate staff
Keep lead-lined container and long-handled forceps in the room
If source dislodges: secure with forceps into the container and notify the radiation safety officer

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does a sealed implant require?

opts.text: Radiation comes from the source — apply time, distance, and shielding (ALARA)
The patient is on strict bedrest to keep the implant from dislodging
A private room with a radiation warning sign is required
Pregnant staff and children should not enter the room
It is fine to spend long, unhurried periods at the bedside
Keep a lead-lined container and long-handled forceps in the room

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: How is exposure minimized and tracked?

opts.text: ALARA = keep staff exposure As Low As Reasonably Achievable
Minimizing time, maximizing distance, and shielding all reduce exposure
Staff wear a dosimetry (film) badge to track cumulative exposure
A dislodged source is handled only with long forceps — never with hands
With a SEALED source, the patient's urine and linens are highly radioactive
Check linens, bedpans, and dressings for a dislodged source before disposal

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: The source is found dislodged on the bed — order the actions

opts.text: Do NOT touch it; grasp it with the long-handled forceps
Place the source into the lead-lined container in the room
Notify the radiation safety officer/physician immediately
Keep everyone back; limit time and proximity to the source
Document the event and how long the source was unsecured

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Which ALARA principle does each action use?

opts.text: Plan and organize care to spend the fewest minutes at the bedside
Rotate the patient's care among several nurses each shift
Gather all supplies before entering so tasks are done quickly
Stand at the foot of the bed or doorway when not giving direct care
Ask visitors to stay several feet away and keep visits brief
Use the bedside lead shield while providing care
Place a dislodged source into the lead-lined container

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm the private room, warning signage, and dosimetry badge
Organize care to minimize time and maximize distance at the bedside
Provide bedrest care and check linens/bedpan for a dislodged source
Screen visitors: no pregnant staff/children, brief visits at a distance
Document care and reinforce the plan with the patient
The nurse clusters care and spends minimal time at the bedside
A dislodged source is secured with forceps into the lead container
Staff wear dosimetry badges and exposure stays within limits
Visitors are screened and kept at a distance for brief visits
A pregnant nurse is assigned to give the patient's direct care
The nurse picks up a dislodged source with gloved hands
The patient is allowed up to ambulate freely in the hall
Linens are discarded without checking for a dislodged source
